blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلضُّحَىٰ (١)
By the morning brightness, (1)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا سَجَىٰ (٢)
and by the night when it is calm! (2)
مَا وَدَّعَكَ رَبُّكَ وَمَا قَلَىٰ (٣)
Your Lord has neither forsaken you, nor is He displeased with you, (3)
وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (٤)
and the Hereafter shall be better for you than the world. (4)
وَلَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضَىٰٓ (٥)
Soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased. (5)
أَلَمْ يَجِدْكَ يَتِيمًا فَـَٔاوَىٰ (٦)
Did He not find you an orphan, and shelter you? (6)
وَوَجَدَكَ ضَآلًّا فَهَدَىٰ (٧)
Did He not find you astray, and guide you? (7)
وَوَجَدَكَ عَآئِلًا فَأَغْنَىٰ (٨)
Did He not find you needy, and enrich you? (8)
فَأَمَّا ٱلْيَتِيمَ فَلَا تَقْهَرْ (٩)
So, as for the orphan, do not oppress him; (9)
وَأَمَّا ٱلسَّآئِلَ فَلَا تَنْهَرْ (١٠)
and as for the beggar, do not chide him; (10)
وَأَمَّا بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ فَحَدِّثْ (١١)
and as for your Lord’s blessing, proclaim it! (11)

(Commentary)

It is said that the revelation (الوحي) — the divine communication from Allah to His prophets — was withheld from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for several days until people began to say, "His Lord has forsaken him." Consequently, this Surah was revealed, and through it, Allah comforted his soul. The Surah carries the possibility of being either Meccan or Medinan.

His saying, the Exalted: والضحى والليل إذا سجى — By the morning brightness, and by the night when it settles, (93:1-2). This is an oath (إقسام). According to al-Mufradat, the morning brightness (الضحى) refers to the spreading of the sun's light and the extension of the day; the time itself is named after this phenomenon. The settling of the night (سجو الليل) refers to its stillness (سكونه), which occurs when its darkness envelops everything.

His saying, the Exalted: ما ودعك ربك وما قلى — Your Lord has not forsaken you, nor does He leave you alone. (93:3). Forsaking (التوديع) means leaving or abandoning. Disliking (القلى), with a kasrah on the qaf, refers to hatred or intense animosity. This verse is the response to the oath (جواب القسم). The correspondence between the light of day and the darkness of night with the descent of revelation and its cessation is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: وللآخرة خير لك من الأولى — And the Hereafter shall be better for you than the world. (93:4). This conveys a sense of progression (الترقي) regarding what the previous verse established—that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remains in a position of honor and divine care (العناية الإلهية). It is as if it were said: "You shall remain in the state of grace and mercy you have enjoyed as long as you are alive in this world, and your life in the Hereafter (الآخرة) — the eternal realm following the resurrection — shall be even better for you than your life in this world (الدنيا) — the present, transient life."

His saying, the Exalted: ولسوف يعطيك ربك فترضى — And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased. (93:5). This is an affirmation and consolidation of His saying: "And the Hereafter shall be better for you than the world." The promise includes an absolute giving (عطاء مطلق) followed by absolute satisfaction (رضي مطلق).

It has also been said that the verse refers to both lives together, rather than the Hereafter alone.

His saying, the Exalted: ألم يجدك يتيماً فآوى — Did He not find you an orphan, and shelter you? (93:6). This verse and the two following it refer to some of the great favors (النعم) Allah bestowed upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). His father died while he was still in his mother's womb; then his mother died when he was two years old; then his grandfather, who was his guardian, died when he was eight years old, after which his uncle took him in and raised him.

It has also been said that "orphan" (اليتيم) here refers to the "unique one" (الوحيد) who has no peer among people, just as one speaks of a "unique pearl" (در يتيم). Thus, the meaning would be: "Did He not find you unique among people and then provide a shelter for the people through you, gathering them around you?"

His saying, the Exalted: ووجدك ضالاً فهدى — Did He not find you astray, and guide you? (93:7). What is meant by "astray" (الضلال) is the absence of guidance (الهداية). The reference to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being "astray" describes his state in himself, independent of Allah's guidance. For neither he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) nor any other creature possesses guidance except through Allah, the Glorified. His soul, in and of itself, was "astray," even if divine guidance was inseparable from it since its inception. Thus, the verse carries the same meaning as His saying, the Exalted: ما كنت تدري ما الكتاب ولا الإيمان — ...You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith... (42:52). Of this same nature is the saying of Moses, as recounted by Allah: فعلتها إذاً وأنا من الضالين — He said, ‘I did that when I was [still] astray.’ (26:20), meaning: "I had not yet been guided by the guidance of the message (الرسالة)."

Close to this is the view that "astray" (الضلال) refers to the departure of knowledge, as in His saying: أن تضل إحداهما فتذكر إحداهما الأخرى — ...so that if one of them errs, the other will remind her... (2:282). This is supported by His saying: وإن كنت من قبله لمن الغافلين — ...though before it you were among the unaware. (12:3).

It has also been said that the meaning is: "He found you lost among a people who did not recognize your right, so He guided them to you and directed them toward you."

Another view is that it refers to him being lost on the way to Mecca when Halimah bint Abi Dhu’ayb was bringing him from the desert to his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib, as has been narrated.

It is also said to refer to the report of him being lost in the mountain passes of Mecca as a child.

Another view is that it refers to the report of him losing his way during his journey to Syria with his uncle Abu Talib in the caravan of Maysarah, the servant of Khadijah.

Other views have been mentioned, but these are weak (ضعيفة) and their weakness is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: ووجدك عائلاً فأغنى — Did He not find you needy, and enrich you? (93:8). The "needy" (العائل) is the poor person who possesses no wealth. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was poor and had no wealth, so Allah enriched him after he married Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (عليها السلام), who gave him her wealth, and she possessed great riches. It has also been said that "enriching" (الإغناء) refers to the answering of his supplication.

His saying, the Exalted: فأما اليتيم فلا تقهر — So as for the orphan, do not oppress him; (93:9). Al-Raghib said: "Oppression (القهر) is both overcoming and humiliating; it is used for either of them." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: وأما السائل فلا تنهر — and as for the beggar, do not chide him; (93:10). Chiding (النهر) is to rebuke and turn away with harshness.

His saying, the Exalted: وأما بنعمة ربك فحدث — and as for your Lord’s blessing, proclaim it. (93:11). Proclaiming the blessing (التحديث بالنعمة) means mentioning it in speech and manifesting it in action, which constitutes gratitude (الشكر) — the recognition and appreciation of divine favors through heart, tongue, and limbs. These commands are general for all people, even though they are addressed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

These three verses are derived from the three verses that precede them, which mention Allah's favors upon him. It is as if it were said: "You have experienced what an orphan feels of humiliation and brokenness, so do not oppress the orphan by humiliating him in his person or his wealth. You have tasted the bitterness of the seeker's need for guidance and the poor man's need for enrichment, so do not rebuke a seeker who asks you to fulfill his need for guidance or livelihood. And you have found that what you possess is a blessing bestowed upon you by your Lord through His generosity, nobility, and mercy; so thank Him for His blessing by proclaiming it and do not conceal it."

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: والضحى, he said: "When the sun rises." Regarding والليل إذا سجى, he said: "When it turns dark."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وما قلى, he said: "He did not hate you."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: ولسوف يعطيك ربك فترضى, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Ibn Mas'ud that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'We are a Household (أهل بيت) for whom Allah has chosen the Hereafter over this world—ولسوف يعطيك ربك فترضى.'"

In the same source, al-Askari in al-Mawa'iz, Ibn La'al, and Ibn al-Najjar narrated from Jabir bin Abdillah that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered upon Fatimah while she was grinding with a hand-mill, wearing a cloak of camel hair. When he looked at her, he said: 'O Fatimah, hasten—and swallow the bitterness of this world for the bliss of the Hereafter tomorrow.' Then Allah revealed: ولسوف يعطيك ربك فترضى."

I say: The narration could mean that this verse alone was revealed after the rest of the Surah had been revealed previously, and was then appended to it; or it could mean that it was revealed alone a second time.

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilyah narrated via Harb bin Shurayh, who said: "I said to Abu Ja'far Muhammad bin Ali bin al-Husayn: 'What do you say about this intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — that the people of Iraq speak of? Is it true?' He said: 'Yes, by Allah! My uncle Muhammad bin al-Hanafiyyah narrated to me from Ali that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I shall intercede for my community until my Lord calls out to me: 'Are you satisfied, O Muhammad?' and I shall say: 'Yes, my Lord, I am satisfied.'"'

Then he turned to me and said: 'O people of Iraq, you say that the most hopeful verse in the Book of Allah is: يا عبادي الذين أسرفوا على أنفسهم لا تقنطوا من رحمة الله إن الله يغفر الذنوب جميعاً — Say, [Allah declares,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins... (39:53).' I said: 'Indeed, we do say that.' He said: 'But we, the People of the Household (أهل البيت), say that the most hopeful verse in the Book of Allah is: ولسوف يعطيك ربك فترضى, which refers to intercession (الشفاعة).'"

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh, with his chain of narration from Ibn al-Jahm, from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام) in the assembly of al-Ma'mun, he said: "Allah the Exalted said to His Prophet Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): ألم يجدك يتيماً فآوى, meaning: 'Did He not find you unique (وحيداً) and then provide a shelter for the people through you?' ووجدك ضالاً, meaning among your people, فهدى, meaning He guided them to recognize you? ووجدك عائلاً فأغنى, meaning He enriched you by making your supplication answered?" Thereupon al-Ma'mun said: "May Allah bless you, O son of the Messenger of Allah."

In the same source, from al-Barqi, with his chain of narration from Amr bin Abi Nasr, who said: "A man from the people of Basra narrated to me, saying: 'I saw al-Husayn bin Ali (عليه السلام) and Abdullah bin Umar circumambulating the House (البيت). I asked Ibn Umar about the saying of Allah the Exalted: وأما بنعمة ربك فحدث. He said: "He commanded him to proclaim what Allah had bestowed upon him."

Then I asked al-Husayn bin Ali (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah the Exalted: وأما بنعمة ربك فحدث. He said: "He commanded him to proclaim what Allah had bestowed upon him of His religion (دين)."'

In al-Durr al-Manthur, from al-Bayhaqi, from al-Hasan bin Ali regarding His saying: وأما بنعمة ربك فحدث, he said: "If you attain some good, then tell your brothers about it."

In the same source, Abu Dawud narrated from Jabir bin Abdillah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: "Whoever is granted a favor and mentions it has indeed shown gratitude for it; and whoever conceals it has indeed been ungrateful for it. And whoever adorns himself with what he has not been given is like one who wears two garments of falsehood."
